occurrenceremarks: Link to Galls observation that were on the stems: Saltbush Woolly Stem Gall Midge (Asphondylia floccosa): https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/255722448 Cattle Saltbush (Atriplex polycarpa), a.k.a. Allscale Saltbush, or Cattle Spinach, is a native, perennial, densely gray–scaly shrub in the Amaranths (Amaranthaceae) family and Saltbush (Chenopodioideae) subfamily that grows up to 2m (79 inches) tall. It grows in alkaline soils in Sagebrush Scrub, Creosote Bush Scrub, and Alkali Sink plant communties. The plant is dioecious bearing male and female parts on different plants. Peak bloom time: July-August. Indigenous people used this plant medicinally and as food source. 6 traditional uses are described here: http://naeb.brit.org/uses/search/?string=Atriplex+polycarpa Anza-Borrego Desert Wildflowers (and more) https://borregowildflowers.org/?type=search&searchtype=S&family=&name=Atriplex%20polycarpa Irene's Cattle Saltbush (Atriplex polycarpa) with closeups: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?taxon_id=58120&user_id=aparrot1&verifiable=any Jepson eFlora https://ucjeps.berkeley.edu/eflora/eflora_display.php?tid=15258 Calflora (includes species distribution map in CA): https://www.calflora.org/app/taxon?crn=1000 Baja California Plant Field Guide, Jon P. Rebman, Norman C. Roberts, 3rd. ed, 2012 (Atriplex, p. 105, species not listed) Shrubs and Trees of the Southern California Deserts. Jim W. Dole and Betty B. Rose, Foot-loose Press, 1996, pp. 18, 47, 118, 120. Native American Ethnobotany: Traditional Native Plant Uses (medicines, foods, fibers, tools): http://naeb.brit.org/uses/search/?string=Atriplex+polycarpa Temalpakh: Cahuilla Indian Knowledge and Usage of Plants, Lowell John Bean and K. Saubel, Malki Museum Press, 1972 (species not listed) xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx Irene's Saltbush (Chenopodioideae) subfamily observations on INaturalist, worldwide: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?taxon_id=518889&user_id=aparrot1&verifiable=any The genus Atriplex (Saltbushes) is variable and widely distributed worldwide with +- 250 species. It includes many desert, seashore, and other halophyte plants that are salt tolerant and grow in poor alkaline soil. Leaves are often simple, fleshy, and white-scaly (whitish or grayish dry air sacs that grow out from the epidermal cells). Jepson eFlora Key to Atriplex (genus): https://ucjeps.berkeley.edu/eflora/eflora_keys.php?key=11490 Taxon Page for Atriplex: https://ucjeps.berkeley.edu/eflora/eflora_display.php?tid=11490 Leaf Shape and Arrangement diagrams: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/e8/Leaf_morphology.svg Plant Identification Terminology: An Illustrated Glossary, 2nd ed., by James Harris and M. It includes many desert, seashore, and other halophyte plants that are salt tolerant and grow in poor alkaline soil. Leaves are often simple, fleshy, and white-scaly (whitish or grayish dry air sacs that grow out from the epidermal cells). Harris, 2022 Native American Ethnobotany: Traditional Native Plant Uses (medicines, foods, fibers, tools): http://naeb.brit.org/ Irene's (aparrot1) Profile Page on INaturalist listing Nature Resources (includes online references with links) for Plants, Birds, Fungi, Lepidoptera, Arachnids, Reptiles, Amphibians, Marine Life, Plant Galls, and more: https://www.inaturalist.org/people/3188668 xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx Coachella Valley Preserve: For thousands of years, particles of sand from the San Bernardino Mountains and Indio Hills washed into the Coachella Valley forming a system of sand dunes. Today, these dunes are part of the Coachella Valley Preserve System, a 20,000-acre sanctuary. The Coachella Valley Preserve also contains several palm oases that sit on top of San Andreas Fault lines. Underground water rises to the surface through these cracks. California Fan Palms (Washingtonia filifera) grow along the cracks where the water seeps up creating a desert oases. Many plant species thrive in the alkaline soil. Coachella Valley sits at the convergence of four vast ecological systems - the Colorado (or Sonoran) Desert, the Mojave Desert, and the coastal and peninsular mountain ranges. https://www.blm.gov/visit/coachella-valley-preserve

occurrenceremarks: Both this and canescens grow at this location.

I spent some time trying to distinguish this species from A. canescens, the Fourwing Saltbrush. Both are woody shrubs with elongated, entire leaves. Fortunately, one of the key features of ploycarpa is that the stem tips become spine-like with age while those of canescens do not. This feature is visible in the pictures and was obvious enough that when I first tried photographing this plant, I thought there were spines coming off the stems.
Seen at Salt Creek
